rights.basis: The poem was created anonymously in the late fourteenth century, many centuries beyond the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 section 12 duration applicable to literary works and anonymous authorship. Modern editorial, encoding, and image layers remain separate.
rights.component: Underlying anonymous late-fourteenth-century poem text only, excluding every modern transcription, edition, markup, interface, and photograph

rights.basis: The anonymous poem's original expression dates to the late fourteenth century and is outside any surviving federal copyright term. The U.S. Copyright Office explains that protection is time-limited and that expiration places a work in the public domain; separate modern editorial and digital contributions are not included in this conclusion.
rights.component: Underlying anonymous late-fourteenth-century poem text only, excluding every modern transcription, edition, markup, interface, and photograph

rights.basis: No repository-level or file-specific license for the copied SJerus XML was found. The artifact is a pinned copy of U-M-origin markup; neither the medieval poem's public-domain status nor OTA's current deposit license is silently applied to these exact repository bytes.
rights.component: The exact copied repository XML bytes and inherited electronic markup, excluding the underlying medieval poem text
rights.jurisdiction: Repository and source-provider terms; United States

entities.description: The checked-in partial XML representation used by this repository.

The thirteen DIV1 elements are supplied hundred-line navigation groups rather than authorial sections.

access.entityLabel: University of Michigan CME item SJerus
notes: The item is the partial U-M representation; it does not supply conventional lines 998-1097.
access.provider: University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative

rights.basis: The 1993 XML publication statement says the item was available through U-M and commercially from OTA but gives no reuse license. The current U-M item was verified only as an access endpoint; no item-specific current rights statement was retrieved. OTA's present CC BY-NC-SA assertion is not transferred to U-M's separate markup and presentation.

rights.jurisdiction: United States; provider statement not located

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item SJerus
notes: The header calls the text unproofread and reports intermittent manuscript emendation, especially in lines 200-600.

rights.component: The three text bitstreams as distributed by the current OTA record
notes: This provider assertion is not transferred automatically to the medieval work, the 1932 printed edition, U-M markup, or manuscript images.

access.accessMethod: Consult the Folger bibliographic record.
access.entityLabel: Kölbing-Day edition of The Siege of Jerusalem (1932)

access.accessMethod: No complete open scan of the exact 1932 edition was found; use a holding library or the controlled 1971 reprint.
access.entityLabel: Kölbing-Day edition of The Siege of Jerusalem (1932)

rights.basis: No source-specific open license or definitive rights statement for the complete 1932 edition was found; editorial and typographical layers must be assessed separately from the medieval text.
rights.component: 1932 editorial text, introduction, notes, glossary, apparatus, and book design
notes: Do not infer public-domain status for every jurisdiction merely from the medieval content or publication date.

bibliographic.citation: Eugen Kölbing and Mabel Day, eds., The Siege of Jerusalem: Edited from MS. Laud Misc. 656 with Variants from All Other Extant MSS., Early English Text Society, Original Series 188 (London: H. Milford, Oxford University Press, 1932), xxxi, 133 pp., frontispiece.
bibliographic.publisher: H. Milford, Oxford University Press, for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 188
bibliographic.title: The Siege of Jerusalem: Edited from MS. Laud Misc. 656 with Variants from All Other Extant MSS.

entities.description: The immediate printed source of Duggan's electronic transcription; based on L and collated against the six other witnesses then known.
entities.label: Kölbing-Day edition of The Siege of Jerusalem (1932)
notes: The subtitle all other extant MSS referred to the seven witnesses known by 1932. P and Ex were not known to the editors.
Kölbing's text and variants for approximately lines 1-580 had begun appearing in 1898; Day revised and completed the edition.

rights.basis: No source-specific open license or definitive rights statement for the complete 1932 edition was found; editorial and typographical layers must be assessed separately from the medieval text.
rights.component: 1932 editorial text, introduction, notes, glossary, apparatus, and book design
notes: Do not infer public-domain status for every jurisdiction merely from the medieval content or publication date.

workPortion.label: Complete edited poem with introduction, notes, and glossary

entities.description: A later reading edition using L as base text and emending it chiefly from P, A, and D; it is a contextual modern edition, not an ancestor of the 1993 U-M encoding.

rights.basis: METS states that its digital editions are copyrighted and intended for individual educational use; its separate CC BY-NC-SA license for downloadable XML does not automatically license the PDF or manuscript images.

access.accessMethod: Download the complete 24-page article PDF from the current NCSU College of Humanities and Social Sciences host.
access.entityLabel: Stinson, Makeres of the Mind (2011)

localCopies.label: Complete Stinson article PDF
notes: The current NCSU URL returns bytes identical to the cached PDF. pdfinfo reports 24 pages and pdftotext extracts pp. 39-62; qpdf succeeds with recoverable shared-object and linearization hint-table warnings, and the bytes were not rewritten.

rights.basis: The complete 2011 article is freely downloadable from the current NCSU host, but neither the PDF nor its host supplies a source-specific open reuse license. Public access is not treated as permission to reuse the article or publisher-formatted PDF.
rights.component: Stinson's article text and the complete NCSU-hosted PDF
rights.jurisdiction: United States and applicable copyright jurisdictions

bibliographic.citation: Timothy L. Stinson, 'Makeres of the Mind: Authorial Intention, Editorial Practice, and The Siege of Jerusalem,' Yearbook of Langland Studies 25 (2011): 39-62, doi:10.1484/J.YLS.1.102108.
bibliographic.title: Makeres of the Mind: Authorial Intention, Editorial Practice, and The Siege of Jerusalem

entities.description: A peer-reviewed study of editorial practice and digital editions that documents the nine-witness tradition, Kölbing-Day's use of L as copy-text, that edition's relatively light emendation, and its lack of access to P and Ex.
entities.label: Stinson, Makeres of the Mind (2011)
notes: This is supporting scholarship about the edition and witness history, not an edition or textual ancestor of the repository artifact.

rights.basis: The complete 2011 article is freely downloadable from the current NCSU host, but neither the PDF nor its host supplies a source-specific open reuse license. Public access is not treated as permission to reuse the article or publisher-formatted PDF.
rights.component: Stinson's article text and the complete NCSU-hosted PDF
rights.jurisdiction: United States and applicable copyright jurisdictions

notes: A generally complete witness; the target text is assigned to the manuscript's fifth hand.

access.entityLabel: Devon Archives, Deposit 2507 binding fragment (Ex)
access.format: JPEG by default; other digital formats may be requested

dateStatements.label: Target-copy date
dateStatements.value: Second half of the fifteenth century

entities.description: A single paper binding strip preserving two discontiguous portions of The Siege of Jerusalem.

holding.notes: Binding fragment discovered during restoration; work portions correspond approximately to Hanna-Lawton lines 985-1017 and 1106-1138.

access.entityLabel: Bodleian Libraries, MS Laud Misc. 656 (L)

dateStatements.label: Current institutional codex date
dateStatements.notes: The official catalog dates the codex more broadly/later than specialist Siege descriptions; both assertions are retained.

entities.description: The oldest generally complete witness and the copy-text of the 1932 and 2004 editions; The Siege of Jerusalem is at fols. 1v-19r.

holding.notes: Target work at fols. 1v-19r; exact work catalog record https://medieval.bodleian.ox.ac.uk/catalog/work_14646.

entities.label: Bodleian Libraries, MS Laud Misc. 656 (L)

notes: Specialist analysis associates the target scribe's language with northwestern Oxfordshire; this is witness provenance, not authorial dialect.

access.accessMethod: No currently working complete public surrogate was verified. Princeton's older two-part full-scan PDF endpoints now return 404 after redirect, and the current catalog record is protected by a traffic-control challenge. Submit the exact catalog URL and shelfmark through Ask Special Collections; its current service accepts digitization orders up to 300 pages and lists USD 2 per image for non-Princeton researchers.
access.entityLabel: Princeton University Library, Taylor Medieval MS. 11 (P)

entities.description: A late-fourteenth-century parchment witness, incomplete at the end; The Siege of Jerusalem is at fols. 104v-110v.

holding.notes: Target work at fols. 104v-110v; current catalog identifier 99111942213506421.

entities.label: Princeton University Library, Taylor Medieval MS. 11 (P)

notes: The copy ends imperfectly around Hanna-Lawton line 1143 and is largely illegible after approximately line 1055.
The witness was unknown to Kölbing and Day and came to scholarly notice after a 1952 sale.

access.accessMethod: No complete public CUDL or ArchiveSearch surrogate for Mm.V.14 was located. Cambridge notes that many medieval manuscripts remain discoverable only through printed catalogs; request priced target or complete-host images through the Cultural Heritage Imaging Laboratory.

notes: A generally complete witness copied by the professional London scribe Richard Frampton.

entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century parchment component; the surviving Siege text at fols. 70r-75v begins imperfectly.

notes: Canvas 1 is the inside upper board and contains no target text.

access.accessMethod: View the official 144-canvas partial scan in Huntington Digital Library or through its IIIF manifest. The sequence covers the complete target work at fols. 205r-216r but only selected portions of the 219-folio host.
access.entityLabel: Huntington Digital Library partial scan of HM 128

localCopies.label: Complete 144-canvas IIIF bundle for the official partial facsimile
notes: The ZIP contains the complete IIIF manifest, a per-image inventory, and all 144 published JPEGs requested at 3000 pixels wide. Every source image is at least 4645 pixels wide, so no image was upscaled. ZIP integrity, 144 per-image SHA-256 values, and image decoding were verified.

workPortion.label: Complete target-work sequence inside the partial host scan
notes: Canvas 106 is the f. 204v-205r opening containing the target incipit; canvas 141 is f. 216r containing the target explicit.

rights.basis: Huntington's current Using the Library page says Huntington Digital Library high-resolution images may be accessed and downloaded free. Its Library Rights and Permissions page does not assert an image license: it says no Huntington permission is required for public-domain material or copyright the Library does not own, asks users to cite specific items, and leaves copyright-holder identification, permission, and fair-use assessment to the researcher. Those statements support access and caching but do not establish that the modern photographs are public domain or openly licensed.
rights.component: The exact 144 manuscript photographs and IIIF derivatives published in Huntington Digital Library for HM 128
notes: Neither the medieval manuscript text's public-domain status nor SJEA's or Bodleian's separate image terms are transferred to this Huntington image layer.

entities.description: An official 144-canvas partial scan of the 219-folio host codex. It includes the complete target-work sequence at fols. 205r-216r but is not a complete facsimile of HM 128.
entities.label: Huntington Digital Library partial scan of HM 128

rights.basis: Huntington's current Using the Library page says Huntington Digital Library high-resolution images may be accessed and downloaded free. Its Library Rights and Permissions page does not assert an image license: it says no Huntington permission is required for public-domain material or copyright the Library does not own, asks users to cite specific items, and leaves copyright-holder identification, permission, and fair-use assessment to the researcher. Those statements support access and caching but do not establish that the modern photographs are public domain or openly licensed.
rights.component: The exact 144 manuscript photographs and IIIF derivatives published in Huntington Digital Library for HM 128
notes: Neither the medieval manuscript text's public-domain status nor SJEA's or Bodleian's separate image terms are transferred to this Huntington image layer.

next_steps: Seek U-M HTI or OTA processing documentation for item U*-1357-A.
Compare any surviving pre-SGML transfer files and conversion logs rather than inferring direction from directory names.
openQuestions.question: What exact production step caused conventional lines 998-1097 to disappear from the U-M/XML representation although they survive in Duggan's complete OTA working transcription?

next_steps: Locate Duggan's transcription documentation, correspondence, or deposited working notes.
Do not create direct U-M-to-witness collation relations until the consulted witnesses can be identified.
openQuestions.question: Which specific manuscript witnesses did Duggan consult for his intermittent emendations, especially in conventional lines 200-600?

next_steps: Check EETS/OUP and research-library digitization services for the 1932 printing rather than treating the controlled 1971 reprint as open.
If a scan is found, verify title page, edition date, completeness, rights, and byte integrity before caching it.
openQuestions.question: Is a complete, lawful, openly downloadable scan of the exact 1932 Kölbing-Day edition available?

next_steps: Ask Bodleian Imaging Services whether a complete surrogate already exists and request pricing and reuse terms for the entire codex or fols. 1v-19r.
Keep the two-canvas Digital Bodleian selection explicitly partial relative to the manuscript.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete digital facsimile of MS Laud Misc. 656, or at least all target folios 1v-19r, be obtained with clear reuse terms?

next_steps: Consult the full codicological arguments behind each date and determine whether they address the same codex component and target hand.
Preserve both attributed statements until their scopes and evidence can be reconciled.
openQuestions.question: How should the current Bodleian beginning-of-fifteenth-century codex date be reconciled with specialist late-fourteenth-century dating of the target Siege copy?

reviewNotes: No direct copied_from relations are asserted among the nine extant witnesses. Modern stemmatic grouping supports textual families but does not identify a surviving manuscript as the physical exemplar of another.
Kölbing-Day's six comparison-witness relations are limited to A, C, D, E, U, and V. P and Ex are later discoveries and are intentionally absent from that edition's upstream chain.

SJEA image terms were not transferred to transcription XML; the Bodleian image license and Huntington provider statements remain separate image layers and were not transferred to the medieval text, physical manuscripts, or one another.
All ignored cache files used by access or evidence records are hard-linked to the canonical main-checkout cache and are not part of the proposed Git commit.

rights.basis: The poem was created anonymously in the late fourteenth century, many centuries beyond the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 section 12 duration applicable to literary works and anonymous authorship. Modern editorial, encoding, and image layers remain separate.
rights.component: Underlying anonymous late-fourteenth-century poem text only, excluding every modern transcription, edition, markup, interface, and photograph

rights.basis: The anonymous poem's original expression dates to the late fourteenth century and is outside any surviving federal copyright term. The U.S. Copyright Office explains that protection is time-limited and that expiration places a work in the public domain; separate modern editorial and digital contributions are not included in this conclusion.
rights.component: Underlying anonymous late-fourteenth-century poem text only, excluding every modern transcription, edition, markup, interface, and photograph

rights.basis: No repository-level or file-specific license for the copied SJerus XML was found. The artifact is a pinned copy of U-M-origin markup; neither the medieval poem's public-domain status nor OTA's current deposit license is silently applied to these exact repository bytes.
rights.component: The exact copied repository XML bytes and inherited electronic markup, excluding the underlying medieval poem text
rights.jurisdiction: Repository and source-provider terms; United States

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a pinned copy of the 1993 University of Michigan CME XML representation of Hoyt N. Duggan's electronic transcription. Duggan worked against Eugen Kölbing and Mabel Day's 1932 EETS OS 188 edition and reported intermittent manuscript emendation, especially in lines 200-600. Kölbing and Day used Bodleian Library MS Laud Misc. 656 (L) as copy-text and collated the six other witnesses then known: A, C, D, E, U, and V. Princeton P and the Exeter binding fragment Ex were discovered after that edition, so its subtitle all other extant MSS must not be read as all nine witnesses known today. The current OTA deposit preserves three text files, including Duggan's complete conventional line sequence 1-1334. By contrast, the U-M and repository XML contains 1,234 L elements labeled 1-997 and 1098-1334: the 100-line gap is a property of this electronic representation, not of the abstract work. The Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive now provides separate descriptions, diplomatic transcriptions, and view-only images for all nine surviving witnesses. A dated institution-by-institution audit found no complete public host-codex facsimile for A, C, D, E, L, P, U, or V; the whole surviving one-strip Ex carrier is viewable through SJEA. Huntington's official partial scan of E includes all target folios 205r-216r, while the Bodleian publishes only a two-image 35mm-slide selection for L. No complete public scan of the 1932 edition was found. Rights and provider terms are stated only for the exact electronic, edition, transcription, and image layers to which the evidence applies.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Probably the 1370s or 1380s; copied in nine surviving witnesses from the late fourteenth through the fifteenth century
catalogSummary.editor: Eugen Kölbing and Mabel Day; electronic transcription by Hoyt N. Duggan
catalogSummary.region: West Riding of Yorkshire, near Barnoldswick and Earby (authorial-dialect localization; composition place not securely established)
catalogSummary.title: The Siege of Jerusalem

catalogingSubject.label: The Siege of Jerusalem
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English alliterative poem conventionally lineated to 1,334 lines. The work is distinct from its nine surviving manuscript witnesses, the Kölbing-Day edition, Hoyt N. Duggan's electronic transcription, the incomplete University of Michigan XML representation, and the separate rhyming Destruction of Jerusalem tradition.

contentParts.label: The Siege of Jerusalem
notes: The thirteen XML divisions are not cataloged as thirteen work sections.
The metrics describe the local representation; the complete work's conventional extent is 1,334 lines.
contentParts.summary: The whole alliterative narrative from the opening in Tiberius's reign through the Roman return after Jerusalem's destruction; the local XML lacks conventional lines 998-1097.

assertion.notes: Modern scholarship favors the 1370s or 1380s; the older c. 1390-1400 date retained in the U-M XML header is not treated as settled.

Specialist descriptions assign both L and P to the late fourteenth century, but their exact priority is not established here. The current Bodleian catalog instead dates the L codex to the beginning of the fifteenth century; the disagreement is preserved in lineage.

dateStatements.display: Late fourteenth century, represented jointly by witnesses L and P under specialist dating
Roman-Jewish war and destruction of Jerusalem, conventionally 66-73 CE and centered on the siege of 70 CE

extent.description: The local XML contains one partial electronic representation of a conventionally 1,334-line poem: 1,234 encoded verse lines in 13 supplied navigation divisions, with line labels 1-997 and 1098-1334. It records 71 printed-page breaks and omits exactly 100 conventional line positions, 998-1097.

notes: The U-M header's c. 1390-1400 creation date is retained as source history but not promoted over later scholarship favoring the 1370s or 1380s.
The Kölbing-Day subtitle all other extant MSS refers to the seven witnesses known to those editors, not all nine manuscripts known today.
Rights and access for medieval text, printed editions, electronic encodings, transcriptions, and manuscript images are separated in the lineage manifest.

next_steps: Review recent linguistic and historical scholarship that postdates the general PCMEP and 2004 METS summaries.
Record competing chronologies as separate attributed assertions if they materially differ from the 1370s-1380s proposal.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's composition be dated or localized more narrowly without converting the inferred authorial dialect or the proposed Bolton Abbey association into a fact?

assertion.notes: The localization is a scholarly reconstruction from authorial-language features.

placeStatements.placeLabel: Barnoldswick-Earby area, West Riding of Yorkshire

scope.description: The 1932 EETS source edition underlying Duggan's transcription
Completion and revision of the 1932 EETS source edition
The electronic transcription represented by OTA 1357 and, incompletely, by the U-M/General Corpus XML

subjects.label: Christian anti-Judaism, antisemitism, and anti-Jewish violence
notes: This heading documents harmful content for responsible discovery; it does not endorse the poem's collective blame, supersessionism, dehumanization, or violence against Jews.

subjects.label: Divine vengeance, punishment, and Christian supersession
notes: The statement describes the poem's ideology rather than accepting its theology or historical claims.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English alliterative romance recasts the Roman siege and destruction of Jerusalem as Christian vengeance for Christ's death. It joins the healing and conversion of Titus and Vespasian to a long military narrative of factional conflict, famine, cannibalism, mass death, enslavement, and the destruction of the Temple. The poem repeatedly blames Jews collectively and uses graphic anti-Jewish violence, dehumanization, and supersessionist theology; those features are documented here for responsible discovery and study, not repeated as historical truth or endorsed.

The complete poem is conventionally lineated to 1,334 lines. The local SJerus XML contains 1,234 L elements with printed line labels 1-997 and 1098-1334; exactly lines 998-1097 are absent. Its thirteen DIV1 wrappers are supplied hundred-line navigation groups rather than intellectual sections. The current OTA deposit also preserves Duggan's complete 1-1334 working transcription, so the gap belongs to the U-M XML representation, not to the abstract work.

Nine manuscript witnesses are currently known: A, C, D, E, Ex, L, P, U, and V. Kölbing and Day knew seven and used L as copy-text; P and the Ex binding fragment became known after their edition. The witness tradition and exact survival gaps belong in the linked lineage manifest and must not be flattened into a claim that all witnesses are complete or that the edition used all nine.

notes: This manuscript rubric is title evidence for one witness, not proof of an authorial title.
titles.supplied_by: Rubric in British Library, Add MS 31042 (witness A)
